The VLAN command specifies the port identifier range. Shows brief info in tabular format. Shows the physical connection info in tabular format. Shows additional statistics, including the tx filtered and rx filte red counters. • Rx filter packets are protocol packets received when the p rotocol is disabled on the switch and there is only one port i n the VLAN. Protocols include OSPF, PIM, RIP, LACP, and LL DP. • An example of a Tx filtered packet would be a multicast pac ket being filtered from going out of the ingress port. Shows statistics rounded to the nearest power of 1000, for exa mple, 1K, 345M, 2G. This is available only in the CLI interface o utput. Shows only non zero statistics. Shows LAG interface information. Continuously monitor interface statistics. Shows loopback interface information.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about VLANs?

VLANs: are primarily used to provide network segmentation at layer 2. VLANs enable the grouping of users by logical function instead of physical location. They make managing bandwidth usage within networks possible by: • Allowing grouping of high-bandwidth users on low-traffic segments Public Source interface selection 167 • Organizing users from different LAN segments according to their need for common resources and individual protocols • Improving traffic control at the edge of networks by separating traffic of different protocol types. • Enhancing network security by creating subnets to control in-band access to specific network resources VLANs are generally assigned on an organizational basis rather than on a physical basis. For example, a network administrator could assign all workstations and servers used by a particular workgroup to the same VLAN, regardless of their physical locations. Hosts in the same VLAN can directly communicate with one another. A router or a Layer 3 switch is required for hosts in different VLANs to communicate with one another.

Default: default. Syntax: copy {primary | secondary} <REMOTE-URL> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

Required. NOTE For more information about hot-patch, see hot -patch. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, how do you configure the DNS client?

To configure the DNS client, follow the documented procedure: 1. Configure one or more DNS name servers with the command ip dns server 2. To resolve DNS requests by appending a domain name to the requests, either configure a single domain 3. To use static name resolution for certain hosts, associate an IP address to a host with the command ip 4. Review your DNS configuration settings with the command show ip dns. Commands shown in the procedure include: VRF Name: vrf_mgmt; VRF Name: vrf_default.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dns domain-name command do?

The ip dns domain-name command configures a domain name that is appended to the DNS request. The domain can be either IPv4 or IPv6. By default, requests are forwarded on the default VRF. If a domain list is defined with the command ip dns domain-list, the domain name defined with this command is ignored. The no form of this command removes the domain name. Specifies the domain name to append to DNS requests. Length: 1 to 256 characters. <DOMAIN-NAME> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: ip dns domain-name <DOMAIN-NAME> [ vrf <VRF-NAME> ].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dns host command do?

The ip dns host command associates a static IP address with a hostname. The DNS client returns this IP address instead of querying a DNS server for an IP address for the hostname. Up to six hosts can be defined. If no VRF is defined, the default VRF is used. The no form of this command removes a static IP address associated with a hostname. host <HOST-NAME> Specifies the name of a host. Length: 1 to 256 characters. Public ip dns host 256 <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexa decimal number from 0 to F. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name. Default: default. Syntax: ip dns host <HOST-NAME>.

source_file: 8400/10_18/Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series).md

source_file: 8400/10_18/Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dns fqdn-resolver refresh-interval command do?

The ip dns fqdn-resolver refresh-interval command sets the refresh interval for FQDN resolvers. The interval specifies the time in seconds after which the FQDN resolver entries are refreshed. The valid range for the interval is between 3600 seconds (1 hour) to 86400 seconds (1 day). For each FQDN entry refresh timer is applicable from the time entry is installed. The no form of this command removes the refresh interval and sets it back to default value which is 1 day. Refresh interval in seconds (default: 86400 seconds). Required. Syntax: [no] ip dns fqdn-resolver refresh-interval <3600-86400>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what restrictions or notes are documented for Guidelines?

For Guidelines, the guide documents the following restrictions, notes, or considerations: • An LLDP must not contain more than one EEE TLV. • LLDP may or may not be enabled on the interfaces supporting EEE. If LLDP is not enabled, it might not be in the optimal operational mode. • EEE TLV should not be exchanged until it negotiates from L1 and it detects peer EEE capabilities. • EEE TLV is disabled by default and enabled only when EEE is active. • EEE and EEE TLV exchange can be enabled or disabled at the interface level.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the lldp command do?

The lldp command enables LLDP support globally on all active interfaces. By default, LLDP is enabled. The no form of this command disables LLDP support globally on all active interfaces. It does not remove any LLDP configuration settings. Syntax: lldp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the no lldp management-address vlan <VLAN-ID> command do?

The no lldp management-address vlan <VLAN-ID> command sets the VLAN whose IPv4 or IPv6 address is advertised as the LLDP management authority. The no form of this command removes the VLAN whose IPv4 or IPv6 address is advertised as the LLDP management authority. The following is the precedence for the management IP address TLV in the LLDP packet (in order): Public lldp management-address vlan 282 • LLDP management-IP-address and management-IPv6-address, if configured. • LLDP management VLAN's IPv4 and IPv6 address, if configured. • Loopback IP address from the smallest configured loopback interface identifier. • Route-only-port IP address (Layer-3 interface) or IP address of the SVI (Layer-2 interface). • OOBM IP address. • Base MAC address of the switch. Specifies the VLAN ID. Range 1-4094.

document_title: Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the lldp management-ip-address <IPV-ADDR> command do?

The lldp management-ip-address <IPV-ADDR> command defines the IP management address of the switch which is sent in the management address TLV. One IPv4 and one IPv6 management address can be configured. If you do not define an LLDP management address, then LLDP uses one of the following (in order): • IP address of the port • IP address of the management interface • Base MAC address of the switch The no form of this command removes the IPv4 management address of the switch. Specifies the management address of the switch as an IPv4 for mat (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal value from 0 to 255. <IPv4-ADDR>. Syntax: lldp management-ip-address <IPV-ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the lldp trap enable command do?

The lldp trap enable command enables sending SNMP traps for LLDP related events from a particular interface. LLDP trap generation is enabled by default on all the interfaces and has to be disabled for interfaces on which traps are not required to be generated. The no form of this command disables the LLDP trap generation. NOTE LLDP trap generation is disabled by default at the global level and must be enabled before any LLDP traps are sent. Syntax: lldp trap enable.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about Thermal Management?

Thermal Management: consists of the fand and tempd daemons, that manage the fans and temperature sensors for the system respectively. They are responsible for monitoring temperatures, adjusting fan speeds to keep the system at nominal temperatures, initiating shutdown sequences for chassis, subsystems, or transceivers during over-temperature conditions, and reporting temperatures, sensor states, fan speeds, and fan states. Subtopics Thermal management support.

document_title: Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about Thermal Shutdown Behavior?

Thermal Shutdown Behavior: Chassis (Switch) Thermal Shutdow - All platforms All platforms Thermal shutdown is a critical sa fety mechanism designed to preve nt hardware damage caused by overheating. When critical tempe rature thresholds are exceeded, th ermal daemons log an event and i nitiate a shutdown sequence to po wer down the affected componen ts gracefully. If a temperature sensor exceeds its critical threshold and the associate d subsystem does not support bei ng powered off, the entire chassis will shut down for a five minute c ool down period. If the critical tem perature is detected again after re boot, the shutdown sequence will repeat. Public.

document_title: Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/Fundamentals Guide (8400 Switch Series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about Transceiver Thermal Management?

Transceiver Thermal Management: Transceiver temperature sensors are visible on REST API calls, SNMP and CLI (show environment temperature). Events logs also are triggered upon thermal status changes, shutdown and recovery. If a transceiver exceeds the temperature threshold and it is hot swapped, the cool time timer is canceled and the transceiver is re-enabled. There is no limit to the number of times a transceiver is thermally disabled and re-enabled if it's continuously above the critical temperature threshold. This is an example of the temperature information on the normal conditions. switch# show environment temperature Temperature information ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- -- Current.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the allow-non-failsafe-updates <num_minutes> command do?

The allow-non-failsafe-updates <num_minutes> command allow non-failsafe updates of programmable devices for the specified number of minutes, or enter 0 to disallow unsafe updates. The maximum permitted value is 120 minutes. Syntax: allow-non-failsafe-updates <num_minutes>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the fabric <SLOT-ID> admin-state command do?

The fabric <SLOT-ID> admin-state command sets the administrative state of the specified fabric module. Specifies the member and slot of the module. For example, to s pecify the module in member 1, slot 2, enter the following: 1/2 Selects the diagnostic administrative state. Network traffic d oes not pass through the module. Selects the down administrative state. Network traffic does not pass through the module. Selects the up administrative state. The module is fully operatio nal. The up state is the default administrative state. <SLOT-ID> diagnostic down up. Syntax: fabric <SLOT-ID> admin-state.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show capacities command do?

The show capacities command shows system capacities and their values for all features or a specific feature. Specifies a feature. For example, aaa or vrrp. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Capacities are expressed in user-understandable terms. Thus they may not map to a specific hardware or software resource or component. They are not intended to define a feature exhaustively. Syntax: show capacities <FEATURE> [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show environment fan command do?

The show environment fan command shows the status information for all fans and fan trays (if present) in the system. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. For fan trays, Status is one of the following values: • ready: The fan tray is operating normally. • fault: The fan tray is in a fault event. The status of the fan tray does not indicate the status of fans. • empty: The fan tray is not installed in the system. For fans: Speed: Indicates the relative speed of the fan based on the nominal speed range of the fan. Values are: Slow: The fan is running at less than 25% of its maximum speed. Syntax: show environment fan [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show environment power-consumption command do?

The show environment power-consumption command displays power consumption information. Displays detailed power consumption information. For VSF supported platforms only. Displays the power consum ption information for the specified VSF member. Range: 1-10. Power consumed values are updated every 5 seconds. The total power consumed is the total power used in a chassis. The power consumed average is calculated from the total power consumed as a running average over a period of time. The average period has a default of 10 minutes. The period can be configured using power-consumption-average-period. On chassis where power input is not supported, power consumed is displayed as Power Usage and PSU Output Power is displayed as N/A. Syntax: show environment power-consumption [detail].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show fabric command do?

The show fabric command shows information about the installed fabrics. Public show fabric 430 Specifies the member and slot of the fabric to show. For exampl e, to show the module in member 1, slot 2, enter the following: 1/2 Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. <SLOT-ID> vsx-peer 8400X 7.2Tbps Fab Mod. Syntax: show fabric [<SLOT-ID>] [vsx-peer].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip errors command do?

The show ip errors command shows harware and software IP error statistics for packets received by the switch since the switch was last booted. If none of the optoinal VRF parameters are included, the show ip errors command displays the current system-wide hardware IP error counters and the default VRF's software IP error counters on the switch Public show ip errors 436 Optional. Display hardware and software IP error counters for a specific VRF. Optional. Display hardware and software IP error counters for al - VRFs IP error info about received packets is collected from each active line card on the switch and is preserved during failover events. Error counts are cleared when the switch is rebooted. Syntax: show ip errors [{vrf <vrf-name>}|all-vrfs].

Failover: This module is a fabric module or a line module, and it is in the process of connecting to the new active management module during a management module failover event. Initializing The module is being initialized. Present The module hardware is installed in the chassis. Public show module 440 Ready The module is available for use. Updating A firmware update is being applied to the module. Examples Showing all installed modules on an 8400 switch: switch# show module.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the usb command do?

The usb command enables the USB ports on the switch. This setting is persistent across switch reboots and management module failovers. Both active and standby management modules are affected by this setting. The no form of this command disables the USB ports. Syntax: usb.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf area command do?

The ip ospf area command runs the OSPF protocol on the interface with the configured IPv4 address for the area specified. The interfaces which have an IP address configured in this network or in a subset of this network, will participate in the OSPF protocol. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol. Specifies the OSPF process Id. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> <AREA-ID> Specifies the OSPF area ID in one of the following formats. • Area identifier in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. Syntax: ip ospf <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID>.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

The syntax of the ip ospf area command is: ip ospf <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID>

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf authentication-key command do?

The ip ospf authentication-key command sets the authentication password used for simple-text authentication. If the password is given in ciphertext it will be decrypted and applied to the protocol. The no form of this command deletes the authentication password used for simple-text authentication. {ciphertext | plaintext} Selects the password format. Specifies the password. <PASSWORD> NOTE When the password is not provided on the command line, plaintext password prompting occurs upon pressing Enter. The entered password characters are masked with asterisks. Syntax: ip ospf authentication-key [{ciphertext | plaintext} <PASSWORD>].

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf cost command do?

The ip ospf cost command sets the cost (metric) associated with a particular interface. The interface cost is used as a parameter to calculate the best routes. The no form of this command sets the cost (metric) associated with a particular interface to the default cost 1. Specifies the interface cost value. Range: 1 to 65535. Default: 1. Public ip ospf cost 146. Syntax: ip ospf cost <INTERFACE-COST>.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf dead-interval command do?

The ip ospf dead-interval command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead if no hello packet is received on the OSPF interface. Public ip ospf dead-interval 147 The no form of this command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead, to the default for the OSPF interface. The default value is 40 seconds (generally 4 times the hello packet interval). Specifies the time interval for the dead interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 40. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: ip ospf dead-interval <INTERVAL>.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf keychain command do?

The ip ospf keychain command sets the key chain for md5 authentication. A key chain configures rotating keys for packet authenticating, reducing the risk of keys being compromised. The no form of this command deletes the key chain used for md5 authentication. Name of key chain to be used for md5 authentication. <KEYCHAIN-NAME>. Syntax: ip ospf keychain <KEYCHAIN-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf passive command do?

The ip ospf passive command configures the interface as an OSPF passive interface. With this setting, the interface participates in OSPF but does not send or receive packets on that interface. Public ip ospf passive 154 The no form of this command resets the interface as active. With this setting, the interface starts sending and receiving OSPF packets. Syntax: ip ospf passive.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf priority command do?

The ip ospf priority command sets the OSPF priority for the interface. The larger the numeric value of the priority, the higher the chances for it to become the designated router. Setting a priority of zero makes the router ineligible to become a designated router or back up designated router. The no form of this command sets the OSPF priority for the interface to the default of 1. Public ip ospf priority 155 Specifies the OSPF priority value. Range: 0 to 255. Default: 1. <PRIORITY-VALUE>. Syntax: ip ospf priority <PRIORITY-VALUE>.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

The syntax of the ip ospf priority command is: ip ospf priority <PRIORITY-VALUE>

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf shutdown command do?

The ip ospf shutdown command disables OSPF on the interface. The interface state changes to Down. It does not remove the interface from the OSPF area. To remove the interface, use the command no ip ospf area. The no form of this command re-enables OSPF on the interface. Syntax: ip ospf shutdown.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip ospf transit-delay command do?

The ip ospf transit-delay command sets the time delay in link state transmission for the OSPF interface. The no form of this command sets the delay in link state transmission to the default of 1 second for the OSPF interface. Specifies the transit delay in seconds. Range: 1 to 3600. Default: 1. Public ip ospf transit-delay 160 Sets the key chain for md5 authentication. A key chain configures rotating keys for packet authenticating, reducing the risk of keys being compromised. Public keychain 161 The no form of this command deletes the key chain used for md5 authentication. Name of key chain to be used for md5 authentication. <KEYCHAIN-NAME> Sets the protocol to advertise a maximum metric so that other routers do not prefer this router as an intermediate hop in their shortest path first (SPF) calculations. Syntax: ip ospf transit-delay <DELAY>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor command do?

The neighbor command this command configures a static OSPFv2 neighbor for point-to-multipoint networks. Static neighbors are required for NBMA (Non-Broadcast Multiple Access) networks, where automatic neighbor discovery through multicast hello packets is not possible. The neighbor IP address should be the IP address of the interface on the neighboring router within the same network segment. Public neighbor 167 The no form of this command removes a previously configured static OSPFv2 neighbor from the point-to- multipoint network configuration. neighbor <neighbor-ip> Static neighbor IP address for point-to-multipoint networks. Syntax: neighbor <neighbor-ip>.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the router ospf command do?

The router ospf command creates an OSPF process (if not created already) on a VRF, and switches to the OSPF router instance context. Up to eight OSPF processes are supported per VRF. The no form of this command removes the OSPF instance. Public router ospf 175 Specifies an OSPF process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies a VRF name for the OSPF process. Default: default. Syntax: router ospf <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what is the syntax of the router ospf command?
The syntax of the router ospf command is: router ospf <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>]

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the router-id command do?

The router-id command sets an ID for the router in an IPv4 address format. The no form of this command unconfigures the router-id for the instance and sets the router-id to the default as follows: the router-id is selected dynamically as equal to the highest loopback address on the router, or the highest active interface if there are no loopback addresses. If no IP address is configured on any interfaces on the router, OSPF will not form an adjacency. Specifies the Router ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. <ROUTER-ADDR>. Syntax: router-id <ROUTER-ADDR>.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what is the syntax of the router-id command?
The syntax of the router-id command is: router-id <ROUTER-ADDR>

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ospf lsdb command do?

The show ip ospf lsdb command shows the OSPF link state database summary for different OSPF LSAs (Link State Advertisement). Use the parameters to get information for a particular LSA. Enter an OSPF process ID to display general OSPF information for a particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> adv-router {<ROUTER-ID>| self} Select to display link states for a particular advertising router. S pecify either a Router ID of the advertising router or specify sel f to show self-originated link states. area <AREA-ID> Select to display information filtered for the specified area in on e of the following formats. • OSPF area identifier in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a d ecimal number from 0 to 255. • OSPF area identifier in decimal format. Value: 0 to 4294967 295. Syntax: show ip ospf [<process-id>] lsdb.

document_title: Ip Routing Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what is the syntax of the show ip ospf lsdb command?
The syntax of the show ip ospf lsdb command is: show ip ospf [<process-id>] lsdb.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ospf routes command do?

The show ip ospf routes command displays OSPF routing information. <IPv4-ADDR> <MASK> Specify an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a deci mal number from 0 to 255. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. Public show ip ospf routes 195 Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME> protected Select to display neighbor information for all VRFs. Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Selects a protected route. Specifying an IPv4 address will select protected route for that prefix. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] routes.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what is the syntax of the show ip ospf routes command?
The syntax of the show ip ospf routes command is: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] routes.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ospf statistics command do?

The show ip ospf statistics command displays OSPF statistics. Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF neighbor informatio n for the particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display OSPF statistics information for all VRFs. Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show ip ospf statistics interface command do?

The show ip ospf statistics interface command displays OSPF statistics for the OSPF-enabled interfaces. <PROCESS-ID> <INTERFACE-NAME> all-vrfs Enter an OSPF process ID to display OSPF-enabled interface statistics information on the specified OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to display information only for the specified interface. Select to display OSPF-enabled interface statistics informatio n for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specify the name of a VRF. Default: default. Public show ip ospf statistics interface 200. Syntax: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics interface [<INTERFACE-NAME>].

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

The syntax of the show ip ospf statistics interface command is: show ip ospf [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics interface [<INTERFACE-NAME>]

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the clear IPv6 ospfv3 statistics command do?

The clear IPv6 ospfv3 statistics command clears the OSPFv3 event statistics. Public clear IPv6 ospfv3 statistics 254 <PROCESS-ID> <INTERFACE-NAME> Specifies the OSPFv3 process ID to clear the statistics for the p articular OSPFv3 process. Range: 1 to 65535. Specifies the OSPFv3 statistics to clear for the specified interfa ce. all-vrfs Select to clear the OSPFv3 statistics for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of a VRF. Syntax: clear IPv6 ospfv3 [<PROCESS-ID>] statistics [interface [<INTERFACE-NAME>]].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 area command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 area command runs the OSPFv3 protocol on the interface for the area specified. To move an interface to a new area, unmap the existing area and then associate a new area with the interface. The no form of this command disables OSPF on the interface and removes the interface from the area. Interfaces which have an IP address configured on the network or in a subset of the network, stop participating in the OSPF protocol Specifies the area ID is one of the following formats. • Area ID in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal num ber from 0 to 255. • Area ID as a decimal value. Range: 0-4294967295. Specifies the OSPFv3 process ID. Range: 1 to 65535. <AREA-ID> <PROCESS-ID>. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID>.

The syntax of the IPv6 ospfv3 area command is: IPv6 ospfv3 <PROCESS-ID> area <AREA-ID>

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 authentication ipsec command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 authentication ipsec command configures IPSec AH authentication. OSPFv3 interfaces that have IPsec configured at the interface context will not use area level IPsec. The no form of this command removes IPsec AH authentication for the specified area. Public IPv6 ospfv3 authentication ipsec 275 Specifies the Security Parameters Index (SPI) to use. The SPI is an identification tag carried in the IPsec AH header. It enables t he receiving OSPF process to select and use the Security Association (SA) from the SA table. The SPI must be unique on the sw itch. Range: 256 to 4294967295. Specifies the authentication type: md5 or sha1. Specifies the key type to use: plaintext (unencrypted), hex-st ring (encrypted) or ciphertext (encrypted). Specifies the authentication key. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 authentication ipsec spi <SPI-INDEX>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 cost command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 cost command sets the cost (metric) associated with a particular interface. The interface cost is used as a parameter to calculate the best routes. The no form of this command sets the cost (metric) associated with a particular interface to the default of 1. Specifies the interface cost value. Range: 1 to 65535. Default: 1. <INTERFACE-COST>. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 cost <INTERFACE-COST>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 dead-interval command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 dead-interval command sets the interval after a neighbor is declared dead when no hello packet is received on the OSPFv3 interface. The no form of this command sets the interval after which a neighbor is declared dead, to the default for the OSPFv3 interface. The default value is 40 seconds (generally four times the hello packet interval). Specifies the time interval for the dead interval, in seconds. Ran ge: 1 to 65535. Default: 40. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 dead-interval <INTERVAL>.

Specifies the authentication key. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 encryption ipsec spi <SPI-INDEX>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 hello-interval command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 hello-interval command sets the time interval between OSPFv3 hello packets for the OSPFv3 interface. The no form of this command sets the time interval between OSPFv3 hello packets to the default for the OSPFv3 interface of 10 seconds. Specifies the time interval between hello packets, in seconds. R ange: 1 to 65535. Default: 10. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 hello-interval <INTERVAL>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 passive command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 passive command configures the interface as an OSPFv3 passive interface. With this setting, the interface participates in the OSPF, but does not send or receive OSPF packets on that interface. The no form of this command resets the interface as active. With this setting, the interface starts sending and receiving OSPF packets. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 passive.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 ospfv3 priority command do?

The IPv6 ospfv3 priority command sets the OSPFv3 priority for the interface. The larger the numeric value of the priority, the higher the chance it will become the designated router. Setting a priority of 0 makes the router ineligible to become a designated router or back up designated router. The no form of this command sets the OSPFv3 priority for the interface to the default of 1. Specifies the OSPFv3 priority value. Default: 1. Range: 0 to 255. <number-value>. Syntax: IPv6 ospfv3 priority <number-value>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show IPv6 ospfv3 border-routers command do?

The show IPv6 ospfv3 border-routers command shows the OSPFv3 routing table entries for Area Border Router (ABR) and Autonomous System Border Router (ASBR). Specifies an OSPFv3 process ID to show the OSPFv3 routing ta ble entries for ABR and ASBR for the particular OSPFv3 proces s. Range: 1 to 65535. Select to show OSPFv3 border router information for all VRFs. Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. <PROCESS-ID> all-vrfs vrf <VRF-NAME>. Syntax: show IPv6 ospfv3 [<PROCESS-ID>] border-routers [all-vrfs | vrf <VRF-NAME>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show IPv6 ospfv3 interface command do?

The show IPv6 ospfv3 interface command shows information about OSPFv3 enabled interfaces. <PROCESS-ID> <INTERFACE-NAME> Specifies an OSPFv3 process ID optionally to show the OSPFv3 enabled interfaces for the particular OSPFv3 process. Range: 1 to 65535. Selects to show information only for the specified OSPFv3-en abled interface. Public show IPv6 ospfv3 interface 306 Include this parameter to display a brief overview of the followi ng OSPF configuration information. Interface: OSPF interface name. • • Area: OSPF area ID. • Cost: The metric OSPF uses to judge a path's feasibility, calc ulated as (reference bandwidth / interface bandwidth). • State: Indicates if the interface is a designated router (Dr) o r a backup designated router (Backup-dr). • Status: Indicates if the interface is up or down. Syntax: show IPv6 ospfv3 [<PROCESS-ID>] interface [<INTERFACE-NAME>] [brief].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show IPv6 ospfv3 lsdb command do?

The show IPv6 ospfv3 lsdb command shows the OSPFv3 link state database summary for different OSPF LSAs (Link State Advertisement). Use the parameters to get information for a particular LSA. Enter an OSPFv3 process ID to display general OSPF informatio n for a particular OSPF process. Range: 1 to 65535. <PROCESS-ID> Public show IPv6 ospfv3 lsdb 309 adv-router {<ROUTER-ID>| self} Select to display link states for a particular advertising router. S pecify either a Router ID of the advertising router or specify sel f to show self-originated link states. area <AREA-ID> Select to display information filtered for the specified area in on e of the following formats. • OSPFv3 area identifier in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. • OSPFv3 area identifier in decimal format. Value: 0 to 42949 67295. Syntax: show IPv6 ospfv3 [<process-id>] lsdb.

Range: 1 to 65535. neighbors <NEIGHBOR-ID> Shows information about a particular neighbor, specified in IP v 4 format (A. B. C. D). interface <INTERFACE-NAME> Shows neighbor information only for the specified interface. detail summary all-vrfs Shows detailed information for the neighbors. Shows summary information for the neighbors. Shows neighbor information for all VRFs. vrf <VRF-NAME> Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Syntax: show IPv6 ospfv3 [<PROCESS-ID>] neighbors [<NEIGHBOR-ID>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the router ospfv3-af command do?

The router ospfv3-af command creates the OSPFv3 AF process (if not created already) and enters the router OSPFv3 AF instance context. Optionally if specified, you can specify a named VRF, or the default VRF if the <vrf-name> is not specified. Maximum of eight OSPFv3 processes are allowed per VRF. The switch allows configuration of OSPFv2, OSPFv3, OSPFv3 IPv4 AF and OSPFv3 IPv6 AF. But at any point only 8 OSPF instances can run per address-family, 8 instances for IPv4 (OSPFv2 + OSPFv3 IPv4 AF) and 8 instances for IPv6 (OSPFv3 + OSPFv3 IPv6 AF). The no form of this command removes the OSPFv3 AF instance. If a VRF is specified, it removes the OSPF instance from the named VRF, or the default VRF if the <var-name> is not specified. Public router ospfv3-af 452 Specifies an OSPFv3 AF process ID. Length: 1 to 65535. Syntax: router ospfv3-af <PROCESS-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>].

Specifies the name of a VRF. Default: default. Syntax: show ospfv3-af [<PROCESS-ID>] IPv6 neighbors [<NEIGHBOR-ID>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor activate command do?

The neighbor activate command this command enables the address-family capability and exchange of information specific to an address family with a BGP neighbor. The no form of this command removes the address-family capability and disables the exchange of routes for the specified address-family with the BGP neighbor. <IP-ADDR> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the nei ghbor. Public neighbor activate 617 Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. <IFNAME> NOTE VPNV4 address-family, peer group, and BFD are not supported for BGP unnumbered. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} activate.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor advertisement-interval command do?

The neighbor advertisement-interval command sets the advertisement interval, which defines the length of time between transmission of BGP routing updates. The no form of this command restores the default value. Default values are 30 seconds for external BGP peer and 5 seconds for internal BGP peer. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a Peer-Group. This is not supported on BGP unnum bered. Public neighbor advertisement-interval 619 Specifies the advertisement interval in seconds. Range: 0 to 60 0. Default: 30 for external BGP peer and 5 for internal BGP peer. <INTERVAL>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDRESS>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} advertisement-interval.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor ao command do?

The neighbor ao command enables TCP Authentication Option (TCP-AO) authentication on a TCP connection between two BGP neighbors. To disable this function, use the no form of this command. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. <keychain-name> Name of the keychain for the neighbor. The keychain allows keys to be configured with different valid li fetimes. This mechanism provides a way for a set of keys to be rotated a nd hence protect against long-lived-key attacks. At any give n time only one key is selected as active-key and keys are vali d for a duration of the defined send-lifetime. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ao <keychain-name>.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor ebgp-multihop command do?

The neighbor ebgp-multihop command enables BGP to establish a session with external peers residing on networks that are not directly connected. By default, BGP can only establish sessions with external BGP peers that are directly connected. If external BGP peer are not connected directly, then ebgp-multihop is enabled to initiate the connection with that external peer. If ebgp-multihop is disabled and external BGP peers are indirectly connected, then BGP peer session is not established. The no form of this command disables the peer ebgp-multihop feature. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> <ttl>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR> | <PEER-GROUP-NAME>} ebgp-multihop <ttl>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor fall-over command do?

The neighbor fall-over command enables BGP fast peering session deactivation. When neighbor fall-over is configured, the BGP process monitors the RIB and if the route to peer is not present in the routing table, it immediately deactivates the peer session without waiting for the hold down timer. It is disabled by default. The no form of this command disables this feature. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDR> <IFNAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Public neighbor fall-over 633 Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. <PEER-GROUP-NAME>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} fall-over.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor graceful-shutdown command do?

The neighbor graceful-shutdown command configures the wait time before shutting down the BGP neighbor session, and can also configure the local preference value to be advertised before graceful shutdown. Public neighbor graceful-shutdown 636 The no form of this command sets the wait time to the default value of 180 seconds and the local- preference value to the default of 0. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255, or IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: x xxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number f rom 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Specifies the local preference value for exporting the iBGP rou tes during a graceful shutdown. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} graceful-shutdown.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor interface command do?

The neighbor interface command configure a BGP neighbor by specifying the interface name on which the neighbor is connected instead of the neighbor’s IP address. When an unnumbered interface is specified to the neighbor command, a static route is auto-provisioned for the neighbor with the unnumbered interface as a destination. A static route is also auto-provisioned if the neighbor IP address is learned via LLDP. NOTE If OSPF is enabled on an unnumbered interface then the auto-provisioning is not supported as the route to the BGP peer is expected to be learned via OSPF. Peer-group is not supported for BGP peering on the IP unnumbered interface. The no form of this command disables this feature. Specifies an IP address. Public neighbor interface 639. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>} interface <ifname>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor passive command do?

The neighbor passive command configures a specific neighbor, or all neighbors in a peer-group, as passive, which means that they will not initiate the TCP session. The no form of this command enables the neighbor to initiate the TCP session. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} passive.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor remove-private-AS command do?

The neighbor remove-private-AS command this command forces the BGP neighbor to drop the private AS numbers. When the outbound update contains a sequence of private AS numbers, this sequence is dropped. If the command is configured for peer-group, then all the neighbors that are part of peer-group will remove the private-AS before sending the BGP update message. The no form of this command allows the private-AS number to be carried in BGP update message. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} remove-private-AS.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor route-reflector-client command do?

The neighbor route-reflector-client command this command configures the router as a BGP route reflector and the specified peer as its client. The no form of this command disables this function. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the neighbor IP address in the IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255, or in the IPv6 form at (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Public neighbor route-reflector-client 666. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} route-reflector-client.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor shutdown command do?

The neighbor shutdown command this command disables the peer session, terminates any active session for the specified neighbor or peer group, and removes all associated routing information. This action can cause the sudden termination of many peering sessions. The no form of this command enables the peer session for the specified neighbor. Specifies an IP address. <IP-ADDRESS> <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME> Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. Public neighbor shutdown 669. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} shutdown.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound command do?

The neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound command this command enables you to generate inbound updates from a neighbor and change and activate BGP policies without clearing the BGP session. Changes in BGP policies require the BGP session to be cleared which can have a large negative impact on network operations. The no form of this command disables this setting. Specifies an IP address. Public neighbor soft-reconfiguration inbound 672 Specifies the unnumbered interface name of the neighbor. Specifies a peer group. This is not supported on BGP unnumbe red. <IFNAME> <PEER-GROUP-NAME>. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} soft-reconfiguration inbound.

The neighbor weight command this command assigns a weight to a neighbor connection. When the weight is applied to a peer-group then all the neighbors that are part of the peer-group will inherit the value configured. The no form of this command removes a weight assignment. Syntax: neighbor {<IP-ADDR>|<IFNAME>|<PEER-GROUP-NAME>} weight <WEIGHT-VALUE>.

You must globally enable the VRRP feature for VRRP virtual router. no router vrrp enable disables VRRP protocol globally but does not remove all VRRP configurations. no router vrrp disable enables VRRP protocol globally. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the apply policy command do?

The apply policy command applies a classifier policy containing a PBR action to an interface. A policy with PBR actions is only applicable to L3/routing interfaces. The no form of this command removes a classifier policy containing a PBR action from an interface. config-if Specifies name of the policy. • Only Layer 3 interfaces are valid for PBR policy application, and only in the routed inbound direction. Syntax: apply policy <POLICY-NAME> routed-in.

source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Routing Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the policy command do?

The policy command creates, modifies, or deletes a classifier policy. A policy contains one or more policy entries ordered and prioritized by sequence numbers. Each entry has an IPv4/IPv6/MAC class and one or more policy actions associated with it. An applied policy processes a packet sequentially against policy entries in the list until the last entry in the list has been evaluated or the packet matches an entry. If a match occurs the related entry, actions are taken. The no form of this command is used to delete a policy or an individual policy entry. Specifies the name of the policy. <POLICY-NAME> <SEQUENCE-NUMBER> Specifies a sequence number for the policy entry. Optional. Ran ge: 1 to 4294967295. comment Stores the remaining entered text as a policy entry comment. Syntax: policy <POLICY-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, how do you configure IRDP?

To configure IRDP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Enable IRDP on an interface with the command ip irdp 2. Set the maximum hold time with the command ip irdp holdtime 3. Set the maximum router advertisement interval with the command ip irdp maxadvertinterv 4. Set the minimum router advertisement interval with the command ip irdp minadvertinterv 5. Set the IRDP preference level with the command ip irdp preference 6. Review IRDP configuration settings with the command show ip irdp.

Optionally, configure IPv6 unicast address prefixes with the command IPv6 nd prefix 3. Optionally, configure support for DNS name resolution with the commands IPv6 nd ra dns ser.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about Interface counters polling?

Interface counters polling: • Agent IP configuration for IPv4 and IPv6 • Header size configuration • Max datagram size configuration • Ingress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Egress sampling for all forwarded traffic (L2, L3) • Enable/Disable sFlow per interface • Support for three remote collectors • A collector can be defined on the default and non-default VRF • Sampling on Ethernet and LAG interfaces • High availability support (sampling continues to work after switch-over) • Source IP support (setting source IP for sFlow datagrams sent to a remote collector) Limitations • Sampling rate cannot be set per interface (global only) • sFlow is not configurable through SNMP. • sFlow ingress is mutually exclusive with mirroring of rx traffic and sFlow egress is mutually exclusive with mirroring of tx traffic. • When rx mirroring and sFlow ingress or tx mirroring and sFlow egress sampling are configured on the same interface, after the boot, hot-swap, or fail-over, either sFlow or mirroring feature will be active on that interface depending on the timing.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the sflow sampling <RATE> command do?

The sflow sampling <RATE> command defines the global sampling rate for sFlow in number of packets. The default sampling rate is 4096, which means that one in every 4096 packets is sampled. A warning message is displayed when the sampling rate is set to less than 4096 and proceeds only after user confirmation. The no form of this command sets the sampling rate to the default value of 4096. sampling <RATE> Specifies the sampling rate. Range: 1 to 1000000000. Default: 4096. Syntax: sflow sampling <RATE>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 dhcp command do?

The IPv6 dhcp command configure to enable DHCPv6 mode on a VLAN. In DHCPv6 Mode, the VLAN will get a DHCP-Server allocated IPv6 address along with DNS and NTP attributes. The no form of the command disables DHCP mode and is supported only on interface VLANs; it is not supported on the management interface. NOTE ZTPv6 attributes are included in the request packet, but they are not utilized by ZTP on the data port. Currently, ZTPv6 attributes are only supported on the management port. Also, IPv6 addresses assigned through DHCPv6 only support a /64 prefix. Syntax: IPv6 dhcp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ip dhcp option command do?

The ip dhcp option command this command enables the DHCP client host name and broadcast flag globally. If the ip dhcp option broadcast-flag command is enabled, then the DHCP offer and ack packets in the DHCP requests will be treated as broadcast packets. These packets will not be forwarded due to the presence of a default static route. Public ip dhcp option 85 The no form of this command globally disables the host name and DHCP client broadcast flag options. NOTE The ip dhcp option broadcast-flag command should be configured before configuring the ip dhcp command. Syntax: ip dhcp option [host-name | broadcast-flag].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command do?

The no ip dhcp preferred-vlan vlan <VLAN-ID> [vrf <VRF-NAME>] command specifies the preferred VLAN for DHCP client options, including DNS, NTP, and Default Gateway. The preferred VLAN is the VLAN used to select DHCP client options within a specified VRF when multiple VLANs have DHCP client enabled in the same VRF. If no VRF is specified, the configuration applies to the default VRF. In cases where the preferred VLAN is not explicitly configured, the lowest VLAN with DHCP client enabled will be automatically selected. The no form of the command removes the preferred VLAN configuration and restores the default behavior.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, how do you configure a DHCPv4 server on a VRF?

To configure a DHCPv4 server on a VRF, follow the documented procedure: 1. Assign the DHCPv4 server to a VRF with the command dhcp-server vrf. This switches to the DHCPv4 2. Define an address pool for the VRF with the command pool. This switches to the DHCPv4 server pool 3. Enable the DHCP server on the VRF with the command enable 4. Configure support for persistent external storage of DHCP settings with the command dhcp-server 5. View DHCPv4 server configuration settings with the command show dhcp-server all-vrfs.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the bootp <REMOTE-URL> command do?

The bootp <REMOTE-URL> command sets the BOOTP options that are returned by the DHCPv4 server for the current pool. BOOTP provides a way to distribute an IP address and boot image file to client stations. The DHCPv4 server returns the IP address and the location of the boot image file, which must be stored on an external TFTP server. The no form of this command disables support for BOOTP. <REMOTE-URL> tftp://{<IP> | <HOST>}/<FILE> Specifies the name and location of a BOOTP file on a TFTP server in the format: • <IP>: Specifies the IP address of the TFTP server hosti ng the file in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal nu mber from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For exa mple, the address 192.169.005.100 becomes 192.168.5.100. Syntax: bootp <REMOTE-URL>.

The default-router <IPv4-ADDR-LIST> command defines up to four default routers for the current DHCPv4 server pool. The no form of this command removes the specified default routers from the pool. Specifies the IP addresses of the default routers in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 255. You can remove leading zeros. For example, the address 192.169.0 05.100 becomes 192.168.5.100. Separate addresses with a space. A maximum of four IP addresses can be defined. <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>. Syntax: default-router <IPv4-ADDR-LIST>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command do?

The static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>] command creates a static binding that associates an IP address in the current pool with a specific MAC address. This causes the DHCPv4 server to only assign the specified IP address to a client station with the specified MAC address. The no form of this command removes the specified binding. Specifies an IP address in IPv4 format (x.x.x.x), where x is a de cimal number from 0 to 255. The IP address must be within the address range defined for the current pool. Specifies a client station MAC address (xx: xx: xx: xx: xx: xx), where x is a hexadecimal number from 0 to F. Specifies the host name of the client station. Range: 1 to 255 pr intable ASCII characters <IPv4-ADDR> mac <MAC-ADDR> hostname <HOST>. Syntax: static-bind {ip <IPv4-ADDR>}|{ mac <MAC-ADDR>} [hostname <HOST>].

section: 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address?
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about 4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? ?

4. Which VLAN is assigned a DHCP address? : The factory default VLAN to which the DHCP address is assigned is VLAN1. Users can configure the VLAN for DHCP allocation on platforms supporting multiple VLAN configurations. Only a single VLAN can support DHCP assignments. 5. Can DHCP assignments be disabled in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch? Use the no ip dhcp command on the required VLAN interface or management VRF port you can disable the DHCP assignments in an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch. A static configuration option is available on both ports. 6. Can IP DHCP switch assignment be carried out on a Routed only Port (RoP)? No, RoP is not supported. Only VLAN and OOBM ports can be allocated an address with DHCP. 7. Can an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch support DHCP IPv6 Allocation? Yes, an HPE Aruba Networking AOS-CX switch can support DHCP IPv6 allocation. However, for Zero Touch Provisioning (ZTP), IPv4 is required. 8.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>] command do?

The dhcp-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>] command configures switch flash storage to be used for backing up client IP bindings (used by DHCP snooping). When flash storage is configured (and external storage is not already configured for this purpose), the switch stores the IP bindings in switch flash storage. When the switch restarts, it reads the IP bindings from the switch flash storage to populate its local cache. Writing the IP bindings to flash storage only occurs after the configured delay and if there has been a change in client IP bindings. Writing is skipped when client IP bindings have not changed since the previous write. Omitting delay <DELAY> sets the default delay of 900 seconds. Syntax: dhcp-snooping flash-storage [delay <DELAY>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcp-snooping trust command do?

The dhcp-snooping trust command enables DHCP snooping trust on the selected port. Only server packets received on trusted ports are forwarded. All the ports are untrusted by default. The no form of the command disables DHCP snooping trust on the selected port. Syntax: dhcp-snooping trust.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the dhcpv6-snooping event-log client command do?

The dhcpv6-snooping event-log client command this command enables or disables DHCPv6 snooping client level event logs that help with client telemetry on a remote management station such as HPE Aruba Networking Central. By default, client level event logs are disabled. The no form of this command disables client-level event logs for DHCPv6 snooping after they are enabled. View these logged DHCPv6 snooping events by issuing the command show events -c dhcpv6-snooping. NOTE For additional information on DHCP-related event logging, please refer to the Event Log Message Reference Guide. Syntax: dhcpv6-snooping event-log client.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the match server access-list <ACL-NAME> command do?

The match server access-list <ACL-NAME> command configures an access list to a DHCPv6 snooping guard policy, enabling the DHCPv6 snooping guard policy to allow or deny the specific DHCP server to assign an IPv6 address. If no filters are applied, DHCP server traffic from any source IP address is allowed in the trusted port. The no form of the command removes the specified access list from the DHCPv6 snooping guard policy. Specifies the name of the IPv6 access list to be matched. <ACL-NAME>. Syntax: match server access-list <ACL-NAME>.

section: 5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about 5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN?

5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN: a. Check if the server is reachable on the same VLAN as the client. b. If the server is on a different L3 network, configure DHCP-Relay to forward the packets between the client and server. 6. Authorized server configurations-When configured, the server assigning IP address should be one of the IP addresses from the authorized server list. In DHCP snooping IPv6, if DHCP Relay is between a test device and a server, the authorised server configuration should include a link to the local IP address of the relay interface. This is because, while forwarding the server's response to the client, it will stamp a link to the local IP address as the source IP of the packet.

topic: 5. Server and client are connected to same the VLAN

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about IP-Binding mismatch in VSX topologies?

IP-Binding mismatch in VSX topologies: 1. Both primary and secondary VSX peers should have the same clock value. If not, there is a possibility of inconsistent ip-bindings that could result in ip-binding sync in a loop. There is no straight-forward way to check the inconsistencies but by looking at the number of bindings and debug log analysis 2. Ensure the ISL state is up and operational. DHCP client IP traffic is dropped at data path 1. This can happen when IP-Source-Lockdown is used along with DHCP snooping. Ensure IP-Bindings are learned for clients that are experiencing traffic failure issues 2. Check the hardware programming state of the IP-Binding entry by IP-Source-Lockdown. Use show i pv4 source-lockdown bindings or show IPv6 source-lockdown bindings commands. Public.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the IPv6 address command do?

The IPv6 address command sets the local IP address of an IPv6 to IPv4 tunnel or of an IPv6 to IPv6 tunnel. This address identifies the tunnel interface for routing. It must be on the same subnet as the tunnel address assigned on the remote device. The no form of this command deletes the local IP address assigned to an IPv6 to IPv4 tunnel. Public IPv6 address 282 Specifies the tunnel IP address in IPv6 format (xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx: xxxx), where x is a hexadecimal numbe r from 0 to F. Specifies the number of bits in the address mask in CIDR format (x), where x is a decimal number from 0 to 32. <IPv6-ADDR> <MASK>. Syntax: IPv6 address <IPv6-ADDR>/<MASK>.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the ttl <COUNT> command do?

The ttl <COUNT> command sets the TTL (time-to-live), also known as the hop count, for tunneled packets. If not configured, the default value of 64 is used for the tunnel. (The hop count of the original packets is not changed.) A maximum of four different TTL values can be used at the same time by all tunnels on the switch. For example, if tunnel-1 has TTL 10, tunnel-2 has TTL 20, tunnel-3 has TTL 30, and tunnel-4 has TTL 40, then tunnel-5 cannot have a unique TTL value, it must reuse one of the values assigned to the other tunnels (10, 20, 30, 40). The no form of this command sets TTL to the default value of 64. Specifies the hop count. Range: 1 to 255. Default: 64. <COUNT>. Syntax: ttl <COUNT>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the vrf attach command do?

The vrf attach command assigns an IP tunnel to a VRF. By default, all tunnels are automatically assigned to the default VRF when they are created. The no form of this command assigns a tunnel to the default VRF (default). Specifies the VRF name to which to assign the tunnel. Syntax: vrf attach <VRF-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the no IPv4 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command do?

The no IPv4 source-binding <VLAN-ID> command adds static IPv4 client source binding information to the switch IP binding database. Although DHCPv4 snooping is often used to dynamically populate the binding database, this command is available for manually adding entries to the switch IP binding database. NOTE Statically configured IP binding information supersedes any dynamically collected binding information for the same client. The no form of this command removes the specified binding that was statically configured with the IPv4 source-binding command. The no form has no effect on bindings that were dynamically configured with DHCPv4 snooping. Specifies the ID of an existing VLAN on which the client is conn ected. Range: 1 to 4094.

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
section: Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A?

Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A: L3-GW(config)# vlan 100 L3-GW(config-vlan-100)# exit L3-GW(config)# interface 1/1/25 L3-GW(config-if)# vlan trunk allowed 100 L3-GW(config-if)# no shutdown L3-GW(config-if)# exit L3-GW(config)# interface vlan 100 L3-GW(config-if-vlan)# ip address 192.168.1.1/24 Now the clients will be able to ping the gateway IP; but will not be able to communicate with each other due to the isolation enforced with private VLAN. As expected, the ping from client 1 to client 2 will fail in the above example, although the clients are in the same subnet.

topic: Step 2: Configuring L3 gateway in switch A

document_title: Ip Services Guide (8400 Switch Series)
section: Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A
source_file: 8400/10_18/IP Services Guide (8400 Switch series).md

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the guide say about Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A?

Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A: If local proxy ARP is enabled in the L3 gateway, the L3 gateway responds to ARP requests for addresses in the same subnet. In other words, the L3 gateway will be doing proxy for ARP resolution. L3-GW(config)# no ip icmp redirect L3-GW(config)# interface vlan 100 L3-GW(config-if-vlan)# ip local-proxy-arp With this, the L3 communication will be enabled between client 1 and client 2. Now the ping from client 1 to client 2 will be successful in the above example. The ARP table in client 1 will be populated with the MAC of L3 gateway; not the MAC of client 2. Similar ARP table entry for client 2 as well. In other words, the L3 gateway is doing proxy for ARP resolution for the local network where clients are isolated at layer 2. Dynamic ARP inspection Dynamic arp inspection is provided as a mechanism for making ARP more secure.

topic: Step 3: Configuring local proxy ARP in switch A

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, how do you configure proxy ARP?

To configure proxy ARP, follow the documented procedure: 1. Switch to configuration context with the command config 2. Switch to an interface with the command interface, or to an interface VLAN with the command i 3. Enable local proxy ARP with the command ip proxy-arp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the arp ip <IP_ADDR> mac <MAC_ADDR> command do?

The arp ip <IP_ADDR> mac <MAC_ADDR> command specifies a permanent static neighbor entry in the ARP table (for IPv4 neighbors). The no form of this command deletes a permanent static neighbor entry from the ARP table. Specifies the IP address of the neighbor or the virtual IP addres s of the cluster in IP format (x.x.x.x), where x is a decimal num ber from 0 to 255.. Range: 4096 to 131072. Default: 131072. Specifies the MAC address of the neighbor or the multicast M AC address in IANA format (xx: xx: xx: xx: xx: xx), where x is a h exadecimal number from 0 to F. Range: 4096 to 131072. Defaul t: 131072. Syntax: arp ip <IP_ADDR> mac <MAC_ADDR>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the clear arp command do?

The clear arp command clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries from the ARP table. If you do not specify any VRF or port parameters, ARP table entries are cleared for the default VRF. Specifies a port on the switch. For example: 1/1/1. ip <A. B. C. D>|all] IPv6 <X: X: : X: X>|all vrf (Optional) Include an IP address to clear neighbor entries for th at specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries for all IP addresses. (Optional) Include an IPv6 address to clear neighbor entries for that specific address, or use the all parameter to clear entries fo r all IPv6 addresses. Clears IPv4 and IPv6 neighbor entries for the specified VRF or for all VRFs. If no VRF is specified he default VRF is cleared. all-vrfs Clear neighbor entries for all VRFs Clear neighbor entries for the specified VRF. Syntax: clear arp.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the debug arp-security <LOG-CATEGORY> [severity <LEVEL>] command do?

The debug arp-security <LOG-CATEGORY> [severity <LEVEL>] command enables ARP security debug logs. If <SEVERITY> is omitted, all severities are logged. The no form of this command disables ARP security debug logs. <LOG-CATEGORY> Selects the ARP security debug log category. Available categori es are: • all: Selects all ARP security debug log categories. • config: Selects the ARP security config debug log category. Public debug arp-security 355 severity <LEVEL> • inspection: Selects the ARP security inspection debug log category. • packet: Selects the ARP security packet debug log categor y. Specifies how to filter the ARP security debug logging by settin g the minimum severity level for which debug logging will be pe rformed. The selected severity level and all severities above (m ore severe) will be included in the logging. Syntax: debug arp-security <LOG-CATEGORY> [severity <LEVEL>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show spanning-tree inconsistent-ports command do?

If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Public show spanning-tree vlan detail 310 Enables and configures the auto re-enable timeout in seconds for all interfaces with BPDU guard enabled. When an interface is disabled after receiving an unauthorized BPDU it will automatically be re-enabled after the timeout expires. messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the destination cpu command do?

The destination cpu command the command causes the mirror session to transmit mirrored packets to the switch CPU. This destination may be configured for multiple sessions, however only one such configured session may be active at a given time. The diagnostic utility Tshark may be used to view and capture packets transmitted to the CPU through this route. Ctrl+C must be entered to terminate a Tshark capture session. More details can be found in the Supportability Guide. The no form of this command will immediately stops mirroring traffic to the CPU, but will not remove any sources from the mirror configuration. Syntax: destination cpu.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the apply qos threshold-profile command do?

The apply qos threshold-profile command applies a threshold profile globally to all Ethernet and LAG interfaces on the switch, or to a specific interface. When applied globally, the specified threshold profile is configured only on Ethernet interfaces and LAGs that do not already have their own schedule profile. Public apply qos threshold-profile 69 The same profile can be applied both globally and locally to an interface. This guarantees that an interface always uses the specified threshold profile, even if the global profile is changed. The no form of this command removes the specified threshold profile from an interface, and causes it to use the global threshold profile. This is the only way to remove a threshold profile override from an interface. A profile can only be deleted once it is no longer applied to any interface. Syntax: apply qos threshold-profile <THRESHOLD-NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos egress-rewrite command do?

The qos egress-rewrite command enables a rewrite of the 802.1Q VLAN priority code point (PCP) or DSCP values for all traffic egressing on the configured interface. The PCP or DHCP value is determined by a globally configured egress priority rewrite map based on the traffic's local priority. Use this command to configure a system-wide mapping from internal local priorities to 802.1Q VLAN priority code point (PCP) or DSCP values. The no form of this command disables egress priority rewrite on the interface. Use show qos egress-rewrite-map to view the current global egress priority rewrite map. Use show qos egress-rewrite-map default to view the factory default egress priority rewrite map. Syntax: qos egress-rewrite [cos|dscp].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos egress-rewrite-map command do?

The qos egress-rewrite-map command configures a system-wide mapping from internal local priority to an 802.1Q VLAN priority code point (PCP) or DSCP value. The qos egress-rewrite cos command allows the PCP value to be rewritten on egress, and the qos egress-rewrite dscp command allows the DSCP value to be rewritten on egress. The local priority associated with a packet affects only its behavior within the switch. By default, it does not influence the priority value written to the packet upon transmission. Public qos egress-rewrite-map 86 This command, along with its no form, accepts a single local priority, a range of local priorities, or a comma-separated list of local priorities and ranges. This provides a convenient method for configuring multiple entries in the map. Syntax: qos egress-rewrite-map local-priority <PRIORITY-RANGE> {cos <PCP-VALUE>}.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos queue-profile command do?

The qos queue-profile command creates a new QoS queue profile and switches to the config-queue context for the profile. Or, if the specified QoS queue profile exists, this command switches to the config-queue context for the profile. A queue profile maps queues to local-priority values. Each profile has eight queues numbered 0 to 7. The larger the queue number, the higher its priority during transmission scheduling. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS queue profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied can be removed. Public qos queue-profile 88 Specifies the name of the QoS queue profile to create or config ure. Range: 1 to 64 alphanumeric characters, including period (.), underscore (_), and hyphen (-). <NAME>. Syntax: qos queue-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos shape command do?

The qos shape command limits the egress bandwidth on an interface to a value that is lower than its line rate. An optional burst value may also be specified. Errors will be generated in the following events: • A user configures a port-shaping value that is greater than 100 percent • A user configures a kbps port-shaping value that is less than the supported minimum kbps value. The supported minimum kbps shaping value can be retrieved using the show capacities command. The no form of this command removes shaping from an interface. <RATE> Specifies the maximum traffic rate in kbps. Range: 1 to 10000 0000. Alternatively, the bandwidth can also be configured as a percentage of link bandwidth. The supported range is 1-100. Default units are kilobits per second. Syntax: qos shape <RATE> [kbps|percent] [burst <SIZE>].

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos threshold-profile command do?

The qos threshold-profile command creates a QoS threshold profile and switches to the config-threshold context for the profile. If the specified threshold profile exists, this command switches to the config-threshold context for the existing profile. The threshold profile determines the action to take when a threshold is exceeded for each queue. A threshold profile is composed of up to 8 queues, numbered from 0 to 7. Each queue defines the action to take when buffer utilization exceeds a specific threshold. Configure queues with the command queue. The no form of this command removes the specified QoS threshold profile. Only profiles that are not currently applied to an interface can be removed. Specifies the name of the QoS threshold profile to create or co nfigure. Syntax: qos threshold-profile <NAME>.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the qos trust command do?

The qos trust command in the config context: • This command sets the trust mode that is globally applied to all interfaces that do not have a trust mode configured. • The no form of this command restores all interfaces that do not currently have a trust mode configured to the default setting. In the config-if context: • This command sets the trust mode override for a specific interface. • The no form of this command clears a trust mode override. The interface then uses the global setting. This is the only way to remove a trust mode override. none Ignores all packet headers. Ingress packets are assigned the l ocal priority and color values configured for CoS map entry 0. Default.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show interface qos command do?

The show interface qos command shows various QoS settings for a specific interface. Specifies the name of an interface on the switch. Format: mem ber/slot/port or lag number. <INTERFACE-NAME>. Syntax: show interface <INTERFACE-NAME> qos.

messages.content: For 8400 AOS-CX 10.18, what does the show qos cos-map command do?

The show qos cos-map command shows the global QoS CoS code point settings, or the factory default settings. Shows the factory default CoS code point settings. Shows the output from the VSX peer switch. If the switches do not have the VSX configuration or the ISL is down, the output f rom the VSX peer switch is not displayed. This parameter is avai lable on switches that support VSX. Syntax: show qos cos-map [default] [vsx-peer].
